message.content:
Adversaries use system checks to detect and evade virtualization and sandbox environments, altering their behavior to avoid detection. These checks involve inspecting system properties, hardware, and software artifacts indicative of virtual machines (VMs) or sandboxes. Common methods include querying Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI), PowerShell, system information, and the registry. Adversaries may also check memory, processes, file systems, and network adapters for VM-specific characteristics. Techniques include searching for specific file paths, registry keys, and hardware properties, such as CPU core count and available memory. Notable malware families like Astaroth, Attor, and Bumblebee employ these tactics to evade detection.

The impact of successful evasion includes prolonged undetected presence, data exfiltration, and further system compromise. Mitigation strategies involve using robust endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools, regularly updating and patching systems, employing behavioral analysis to detect anomalies, and using deception techniques like honey tokens to identify adversary reconnaissance. Additionally, implementing strict access controls and monitoring for unusual system queries can help detect and prevent these evasion techniques.
